policy_statement: Require waste reduction plans for all large events.
mechanism_description: Mandating waste reduction planning for large events reduces and diverts waste from landfills, which lowers methane emissions from landfill decomposition.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management practice change (↓ landfilling, ↑ diversion) → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Municipal solid waste generation, collection, and disposal practices shift away from landfilling toward source reduction, reuse, recycling, composting/anaerobic digestion, and other diversion pathways.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Less biodegradable material is placed in anaerobic landfill environments, reducing methanogenesis and subsequent methane emissions (including reduced reliance on imperfect landfill gas capture).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane (and associated CO₂e) emissions from the waste sector, reducing near-term radiative forcing and overall GHG inventory.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided landfill methane emissions (reduced GHG emissions) from diverting waste away from landfilling.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Policies increase diversion from landfill, which reduces methane generation/emissions—this is a direct GHG mitigation effect.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some policies may also improve resource efficiency (less virgin material production) or reduce local pollution, the mechanism as defined centers on avoiding landfill methane, which is unequivocally mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If diversion occurs via waste-to-energy incineration, methane avoidance may still occur but CO₂ emissions can rise; if diversion is primarily recycling, upstream manufacturing emissions reductions may rival methane avoidance. These do not change the mechanism’s dominant stated pathway (landfill methane avoidance) for classification at this stage.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces landfilling through waste prevention, recycling, or organics diversion will, by the mechanism definition, reduce landfill methane relative to baseline and thus fits Mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy is best characterized secondarily as recycling-driven resource efficiency, organics composting/AD, landfill policy, or behavior change depends on policy-level details and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some diversion strategies (e.g., waste-to-energy/incineration, export of waste to other landfills, or increased transport distances) can dilute or offset net GHG benefits; however, the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly methane avoidance from reduced landfilling, which remains a mitigation classification.
co_benefits: Reduced litter and local pollution; lower disposal costs; improved public cleanliness at events.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a planning/strategy instrument if it only requires submission of plans without enforceable performance outcomes; absent that detail, “Require” implies a mandate with compliance expectations.
